mappings.rationaleAI: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems prove any sufficiently powerful formal system is incomplete or inconsistent (B.3), directly engaging set theory and natural-number arithmetic (A.5).
Surveys formal logic from Aristotle's syllogistic (A.3) through propositional (A.1) and predicate calculus (A.2) to Frege, covering Gödel's metalogical limits (B.3).
Russell's Paradox exposed contradictions in naive set theory (A.5), motivating type theory and formal systems (B.2), with Principia addressing completeness and consistency (B.3).
Game theory formalizes strategic decision-making through models of rational action under uncertainty, corresponding to practical logic (C.3).

mappings.episodeTitle: Goethe and the Science of the Enlightenment
mappings.rationaleAI: Cantor's set theory proving different sizes of infinity and the continuum hypothesis are core to set theory (A.5); undecidability of the continuum hypothesis is a landmark metalogical result (B.3).
Ordinary language philosophy represents a systematic critique of forms of reasoning (C.1) and argumentation grounded in linguistic practice (C.1.a).
Scientific method concerns critique of reasoning (C.1): Bacon's induction vs Aristotelian deduction, Popper's falsifiability demarcating science from pseudoscience (C.1.b).
Theories of truth are central to epistemic logic (C.2), dealing with concepts of belief, knowledge, and assertion.
Euclid's Elements established the axiomatic-deductive paradigm for formal systems (B.2); number theory connects to natural-number arithmetic (A.5).
Zeno's paradoxes stimulated development of set theory and rigorous treatment of natural-number arithmetic (A.5).
Godel's formalization of the ontological argument in modal logic connects to formal logic and its applications (A, B).
